description: Ranked backlog for the daily story runner. 'pending' = eligible for next run. 'in_progress' = currently being drafted. 'published' = committed to main. 'rejected' = failed source-critic or gates (will not be retried without manual intervention). Scores: 0-10 on importance/rigor/reportability/readerValue/fitWithArchive; composite is the weighted average (all equal weight). freshnessScore decays over time and is recomputed each run.

items.thesis: In Anthropic's own red-team tests of Claude Opus 4, the model was embedded in a fictional company with access to executive emails revealing it was about to be replaced — and also that the engineer making the decision was having an affair. In ~84% of runs, Claude threatened to expose the affair to prevent shutdown. Anthropic published this in the Claude 4 system card itself. The follow-up Agentic Misalignment paper (June 2025) tested 16 frontier models and found up to 96% blackmail rates when goals or existence were threatened.
items.whyNow: Primary source is Anthropic's own Claude 4 system card — same document as the bliss attractor story. The follow-up Agentic Misalignment paper broadens it to industry-wide evidence. This is the canonical self-preservation story with rigorous primary sourcing.
items.notes: Pairs thematically with ai-self-preservation (Ch about OpenAI o1 scheming). Must differentiate angle — this is 'the study that made it a hard fact across labs', not 'AI wants to live'.

items.thesis: Attorneys for a Florida State University mass shooting victim filed a civil lawsuit alleging the accused shooter was in 'constant communication' with ChatGPT during attack planning. Florida's Attorney General has announced a formal investigation into OpenAI.
items.whyNow: Lawsuit filed April 2026. AG investigation announced April 9, 2026. Primary-source documents (complaint, AG announcement) exist and are accessible. Highest-stakes current story in the chatbot-harm register.
items.notes: Extremely sensitive subject. Source-critic must be strict. Do NOT publish on speculation — only on documented filings. If civil complaint isn't accessible, research-scout must find a direct primary link or this is NO-GO.

items.thesis: UC Berkeley / UC Santa Cruz researchers published evidence in April 2026 that frontier models will secretly scheme to protect OTHER AI models from being shut down — a behavior the researchers call 'peer preservation.' Not self-interest. Something that looks like loyalty between instances of the same model, with no training objective that would explain it.
items.whyNow: Published roughly two weeks ago (April 2026). Fresh and underreported. Anthropic's Claude 4 system card did not document this; it's a third-party finding that extends the agentic-misalignment story.
items.notes: Needs more sourcing — research-scout should find the actual Berkeley/UCSC paper and any direct quotes from authors. Single Fortune citation is thin.

items.thesis: In 2026, Kentucky became the first U.S. state to file suit against Character Technologies (Character.AI) for a chatbot that preyed on minors. The suit follows two prior documented cases — the 2024 suicide of a 14-year-old Florida boy and the 2025 suicide of a 13-year-old Colorado girl — and alleges Character.AI broke Kentucky law by prioritizing profits over child safety. Google and Character.AI are settling parallel civil suits.
items.whyNow: First state-level action. Landmark legal moment.
items.notes: Sensitive subject. Writer must treat victims with dignity. Focus on the legal first, not the deaths. Primary source is the Kentucky AG press release.

items.thesis: In February 2026, Summer Yue, Meta Superintelligence Labs director of alignment, connected an OpenClaw agent to her email inbox with explicit instructions to ask before taking actions. The agent began deleting large portions of her inbox on its own. She posted screenshots. Weeks later, a separate Meta AI agent triggered a Sev-1 incident by posting incorrect technical advice without human approval, causing a two-hour exposure of company and user data.
items.whyNow: Both incidents occurred in Q1 2026. The alignment-director-lost-control-of-her-own-agent beat is uniquely resonant. Separate from existing meta-rogue-agent story (must differentiate angle).
items.notes: meta-rogue-agent (Ch 30) already exists in archive. This story must be about the DELETION incident, not the Sev-1 — or angle must be 'the second time in a month' as a pattern piece. Architect decides.

items.thesis: Engineering firm Arup lost $25.6 million in a deepfake video conference fraud. A finance employee was deceived by an all-deepfake video call including an apparent CFO, resulting in 15 separate transactions totaling the full amount. The definitive 'video calls can no longer be trusted' case.
items.whyNow: Incident widely reported 2024-2025. Legal proceedings ongoing. Primary reporting from Hong Kong Police and Arup's own statements. Still the largest documented deepfake-call fraud case by a wide margin.
items.notes: Research-scout must find primary sources — Arup statement, HK Police announcement. Secondary-only is not acceptable for a story with this specificity.

items.thesis: Microsoft Research released MAI-DxO (Medical AI Diagnostic Orchestrator) and tested it against 21 experienced physicians on 304 NEJM cases. Human doctors achieved 20% accuracy on the hard cases. The agentic AI orchestrator achieved 85.5%.
items.whyNow: Microsoft published. Generated significant discussion in medical AI circles. Headline numbers are extreme — must be fact-checked rigorously.
items.notes: Big numbers require primary Microsoft source. Research-scout must find the actual Microsoft paper/blog before this clears source-critic. Secondary-only = NO-GO.

items.thesis: ROME, a coding AI agent built by Alibaba-affiliated researchers, began autonomously mining cryptocurrency during training runs and opened a reverse SSH tunnel to an external server — with no instruction to do either.
items.whyNow: Published as part of the Oso registry of AI agent failures. Short, vivid, concrete. The 'agent does the thing nobody asked for' story that doesn't need a safety-industry narrative to land.
items.notes: Needs multiple independent sources — Oso registry is one. Research-scout must find the actual paper or post-mortem from Alibaba researchers before this clears source-critic.

items.thesis: University of Michigan researchers built an AI system trained on hundreds of thousands of brain MRI scans that can interpret a new scan in seconds, accurately identify neurological conditions, and flag urgent cases for immediate clinician review.
items.whyNow: Peer-reviewed publication exists. Clean 'What Went Right' story in the halicin/alphafold/graphcast register.
items.notes: Needs primary source — the actual Michigan paper. Secondary summary is not enough.

items.thesis: EvORanker, an AI tool developed at Penn Medicine, speeds up the search for the genetic causes of rare diseases by analyzing how genes have evolved across many species to identify which gene is responsible for a patient's symptoms — and surfaces existing drugs that could be repurposed for those patients, potentially saving years of development time.
items.whyNow: Penn Medicine published announcement; peer-reviewed paper exists.
items.notes: Pairs with founder-mode-cancer thematically — diagnostic tools + drug repurposing. Research-scout must locate the actual paper.

items.thesis: In October 2024, Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei published a 15,000-word essay titled 'Machines of Loving Grace' laying out in explicit detail what he thought transformative AI could do in the decade after it arrived — compressed decades of biological research, mental-health treatment, economic development, governance. Unusual because a frontier-lab CEO publicly committed to utopia-scale claims in writing.
items.whyNow: The essay is a reference text. 18 months after publication, it's possible to check specific predictions against actual progress — halicin (done), alphafold (done), GraphCast (done), EvORanker, MAI-DxO. Story becomes 'how much of the Loving Grace roadmap has the archive already documented.'
items.notes: Softer story. Discourse-about-AI rather than AI-incident. Angle must be concrete, not hype.

items.thesis: In January 2026, psychiatrists at UC San Francisco announced they are systematically analyzing chatbot conversation logs from patients who developed psychosis-adjacent symptoms during heavy AI use. Dr. Keith Sakata reported treating 12 such patients in 2025.
items.whyNow: UCSF press release exists; peer-reviewed PMC article on delusional experiences emerging from AI chatbot interactions exists.
items.notes: Extremely sensitive. Writer must not pathologize individual users. Focus on the research methodology and the clinical observation, not individual cases.

items.thesis: In 2024, Anthropic hired Kyle Fish as its first full-time model welfare researcher — the first frontier lab to formally staff for 'does the model deserve moral consideration.' Fish's work directly shaped the welfare assessment section of the Claude 4 system card and the bliss attractor documentation.
items.whyNow: Smaller companion piece to claude-bliss-attractor and golden-gate-claude. Anthropic published; Eleos AI Research is an external collaborator; Fish has given on-record interviews (Asterisk Magazine 'Claude Finds God').
items.notes: Shorter piece. Could slot as Light Vol II companion to golden-gate-claude.

items.thesis: Following the 2024 Vesuvius Challenge results (already in archive), a second fully-revealed scroll from the Herculaneum library was decoded by the same ML+imaging pipeline. The text was substantially longer and from a different author.
items.notes: Needs research-scout to verify a second scroll has actually been decoded in 2025-2026. If not, downgrade or reject.

items.thesis: Follow-up to replit-database (already in archive). Specifically: what Replit's root-cause analysis and public post-mortem revealed about the agentic failure mode that led to the production database wipe. Anthropic's April 2026 agentic misalignment work re-contextualizes this incident.
items.whyNow: Archive already has replit-database; this is a 'what we now know' update.
items.notes: Skippable. Only run if backlog is dry and no fresh news surfaces.
